title: OpenAI launches 'Dreaming' — a background memory system for ChatGPT
subtitle: ChatGPT now synthesizes long-term context between sessions rather than waiting for explicit save commands, with a 5x compute reduction enabling rollout to free users.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI rolled out a new ChatGPT memory architecture called Dreaming on June 4, 2026, starting with Plus and Pro users in the United States. The system runs as a background process between conversations, synthesizing preferences, constraints, and project context from prior chats into a coherent user profile, instead of relying on users to say 'remember this.' OpenAI claims the new architecture pushed factual recall on its internal benchmark to 82.8%, up from 67.9% in 2025 and 41.5% in 2024.

The architectural choice matters more than the name. Previous ChatGPT memory worked like a sticky-note pile — discrete bullets the model could read back. Dreaming consolidates those notes into a running model of the user, decays stale information, and exposes the result in a reviewable summary page where users can edit or delete entries. OpenAI also said engineering work cut the compute needed to serve dreaming by roughly 5x, which is what makes the rollout to free users — over the coming weeks — economically feasible.

Persistent memory has been the missing ingredient for assistant-style AI products for the better part of two years. Anthropic shipped Projects-level memory for Claude; Google added cross-session context to Gemini. The race has shifted from 'can the model remember' to 'does the model build a useful long-term picture without becoming creepy or stale.' Dreaming is OpenAI's bet that the answer is automated synthesis with strong user controls, rather than user-curated facts.

A note for learners: when you read a benchmark like '82.8% factual recall' for a memory system, ask what's being measured and against what baseline. Recall on a held-out test set is not the same as recall on your own conversations, and self-reported benchmarks from labs rarely match independent evaluations. The right move is to try the feature on a real workflow you care about, then check whether it remembers what you actually want it to.

sources.title: Dreaming: Better memory for a more helpful ChatGPT — OpenAI
